what_it_does: Acceptance test file for the routine_schedule subsystem (S-R9.1). It verifies that routine cadences are rendered into valid systemd .service and .timer units, that bad cadences fail loudly with ScheduleError, and that generate_units writes files plus emits an arm command and services.json entry without actually enabling the units.
observations: Uses a hand-rolled PASS/FAIL harness with check() and raises() helpers rather than pytest/unittest.
Imports Routine, routine_registry, render_units, generate_units, _timer_directive, unit_names, and ScheduleError from runtime.
Tests OnCalendar cadence rendering, every:<seconds> interval rendering, and fail-loud cases: missing cadence, unparseable cadence, and every:0.

Verifies generate_units writes .service and .timer files, produces a services.json entry with group=jobs, and returns an arm_command containing 'systemctl --user enable' and a note 'GENERATED, NOT ARMED'.
Includes a final check that the shipped self_status routine from routine_registry renders successfully.
standouts: Explicit GENERATES-NOT-ARMS policy: the code emits the enable command but does not execute it.
Fail-loud validation via ScheduleError for unschedulable or zero-interval cadences.
Acceptance-style test that exercises the full render/generate path end-to-end.
conventions: Docstring prefixes the file with a requirement identifier (S-R9.1) and a clear scope boundary (no systemctl, no arming).
Manual sys.path insertion to locate the runtime package.
Test functions use a simple boolean check helper and a raises helper for exception assertions.
concerns: Manual test harness is non-standard and lacks rich assertion reporting.
Tests a private helper _timer_directive directly, coupling tests to internal implementation.
Relies on the shipped self_status routine being present in routine_registry, which may make the test sensitive to sample data.
notes: An acceptance test that proves routine cadences become systemd units and that arming is deliberately left to the operator.
questions: What is the exact definition and source of requirement S-R9.1?
What does runtime.routine_runner do and how is it invoked in production?
What is the full schema of the services.json entry produced by generate_units?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the file content: it tests cadence-to-systemd rendering, fail-loud behavior, and generate-not-arm semantics.
apparent_themes: Acceptance testing without external test framework
intention_signals: The file appears to enforce a design where scheduling configuration is validated early, rendered into portable systemd artifacts, and arming is an explicit, auditable operator step rather than an automatic side effect.
novelty: The explicit GENERATES-NOT-ARMS acceptance criterion is unusual: it treats emitting an enable command as the expected behavior rather than a missing feature.
contribution: Provides acceptance-level coverage that the schedule renderer produces correct systemd units, rejects bad cadences, and defers unit enablement to an operator command, preserving a clear separation between generation and arming.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance tests for routine_schedule that validate systemd unit rendering from cadences, fail-loud invalid cadence handling, and generate-not-arm behavior.
intention_for_embedding: To prove S-R9.1 schedule generation works correctly and intentionally stops short of enabling systemd units.

symbols.does: Records a named test result as PASS or FAIL and prints the outcome.
symbols.description: The check helper takes a test name, a boolean condition, and an optional detail string. It appends the name to PASS or FAIL, prints the result, and includes the detail only on failure.

symbols.does: Returns whether a callable raises ScheduleError, optionally matching a substring.
symbols.description: The raises helper runs a zero-argument callable, catches ScheduleError, and checks whether the requested substring appears in the exception message. It returns False for any other exception or if the substring is absent.
